item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Kinda amazed at how many people in this discussion DESPERATELY want the Foundation to be made of terrible human beings. Maybe I'm just too much of an optimist for a collaborative horror project about What If It Was All Just Fucked Up Things, but I'm generally pretty happy with this view on the humanity of the Foundation as a whole. I don't think they all necessarily need to be frantically willing to sacrifice thousands of people a day. I honestly think it's pretty okay if, for the length of a single SCP article, they're good people at heart who are forced to decimate the population they work so hard to protect. Big +1 and if I could upvote again, I would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know right!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember back when I was an accountless lurker I wanted to vote for this one, but didn't have an account, so. This paints a very well drawn picture of a grim dark foundation, and gives a clear explanation of why D's are subjected to what they are, and above it all; this article was executed very well.

And stonefish was right; you made the d-boy cliches absolutely horrifying.

+1, you've earned it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm… giving this a downvote, because I feel like this completely handwaves something I've always felt to be kind of… the entire point of the D-Class. They're there as evidence of the horrors that humans can wreak on one another. They're there for things like the (poorly handled) Stanford Prison Experiment, but really… any case where one group is encouraged to ignore empathetic feelings for other humans. We see it being done to humanoid SCPs, even the nicest of them, and so to see it with the D-Class makes sense. To know that humans have done this, on their own, time and time again and before over millennia - to slaves, to prisoners, to people who believe something different. Hell, even now - Guantanamo Bay, the anti-semitic protests going on, fucking. Trump in the White House clearly lacking empathy for everyone. All of those people who have been accused of or have definitely committed sexual assault. The horror of what humans will willingly and knowingly do to humans I've always felt has been a defining characteristic of this site and the whole principles behind SCP as a project. To attribute it all to an SCP its… "Oh yes, I tortured this D-Class to death but it wasn't me. The SCP made me do it!" Or… "It's not my fault! I was only obeying the SCP!"

I don't know. But to me, this feels poorly handled, poorly explained, and as though it completely and deliberately undermines part of what was the most horrorful of the aspects of this site and this project - that humans given power often can and will commit horrors against other humans. And the researchers seem to know this, given the preparations mentioned in a number of other posts to find people fitting certain psychological profiles or needing psychological help.

To me, at leas, this article feels like it undermines part of the entire horror of the site, by passing it off as "an SCP did it" when its worse knowing that humans in their right minds would do those things anyway. At least in my opinion.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
We can stick with the first then.

When in doubt…

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I am okay with the first and lukewarm in the second.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so this one being a D-Class Contest entry makes it a bit tricky to crosslink, due to the goal being to do a unique take on the D-Class, and stand against status quo canon. That being said, a few places where links can be included are here. The first one would be a link to History at the mention of the D-Class barracks. Given that is a tale about the D-Class barracks, and that its been a while since the contest concluded, such a link wouldn't be too far out of line. Additionally, the mention of the O5's being infected could be linked to the O5 Dossier to give the reader insight into who exactly it is that might be infected. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-2959-A is a wing or floor (in certain cases, entire buildings) that serve as the “D-Class barracks.” SCP-2959-A fully incorporates itself into Foundation sites when any involved personnel are infected by an SCP-2959 instance or an affected Foundation employee. The materialization of SCP-2959-A has been shown to occur instantaneously. SCP-2959-A hold all necessary equipment for care, eradication, and control of SCP-2959. All site personnel view SCP-2959-A as always having been a part of a building.

To this:

SCP-2959-A is a wing or floor (in certain cases, entire buildings) that serve as the “D-Class barracks.” SCP-2959-A fully incorporates itself into Foundation sites when any involved personnel are infected by an SCP-2959 instance or an affected Foundation employee. The materialization of SCP-2959-A has been shown to occur instantaneously. SCP-2959-A hold all necessary equipment for care, eradication, and control of SCP-2959. All site personnel view SCP-2959-A as always having been a part of a building.

Changing this:

And here's where we stand. Most of the O5s have been infected. They're being kept on, of course, but we're trying to steer them into retirement as soon as possible. We only have a handful of personnel that we could save when we saw things were going to hell. But we have a few researchers who were out sick and agents lucky enough to have been off the grid. It's only us now.

To this:

And here's where we stand. Most of the O5s have been infected. They're being kept on, of course, but we're trying to steer them into retirement as soon as possible. We only have a handful of personnel that we could save when we saw things were going to hell. But we have a few researchers who were out sick and agents lucky enough to have been off the grid. It's only us now.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So did I, I mean they might as well just make D-class part of some of the scps diets considering how many they sacrifice to them already

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All this from a comment about a single word in the entire article… Wow…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I down voted as I really dislike the idea. This basically eradicates the "we do what we have to, to save humanity" aspect from the whole concept of the foundation. I mean it's well written and all but I just really dislike this. Not everything has to be a SCP. Some things are simply done because they are necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show opinion post
- Hide click HERE to close

This SCP is very on-the-nose. It feels like a kind of meta-commentary while also explaining in-universe why D-Class are subjected to terrible things. It’s also a neat explanation and handwave for people who don’t LIKE D-Class as an element to be able to point towards and say "Hey, this is where D-Class come from", and combining all the older D-Class stereotypes into one place to basically make everyone win.

The Foundation, the O5s, the people who 'become' D-Class, researchers who need them, everyone.

I mean… well, it’s an overarching, deeply ingrained 'infection' creating test subjects, but eh. Silver linings.

In a way it’s a perfect entry for the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You get a begrudging upvote because while I prefer my Foundation with actual non-anomalous D-class, there's room for other interpretations and you pull this off pretty well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd say it was a deliberate authorial choice to repeat the madness mantra of "what X did, what X were" to suggest that the anomaly is at least a little bit formerly human. Its even the title of the thing on the mainlist. That's what I got from it, anyway, and it certainly gives the whole thing a moral kind of dimension when it comes to the plausible humanity of the D-class here.

That said, while I initially had reservations along your lines1, I ended up upvoting because the article itself was well-written and enjoyable, setting aside its place in the general tone of the site and its implications for lore. I've excused worse assumptions for less.

Footnotes
1. particularly because it's whitewashing the antiheroes I came and joined the site for - it's like modern Batman suddenly turning around and becoming Adam West because "it's all just a bad dream!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is way, way too dependent on meta-commentary for me to read this and take it seriously. In a weird way, it actually reinforces a lot of the dumber portrayals of human experimentation on the wiki, because now someone can look at that and go "see, it's because of a skip", when in fact we should probably be trying to portray that aspect of things in a more realistic, non-stupid way. This acknowledges the silliness of past practices while providing cover.

This also simultaneously asks the reader to be horrified by the willingness of the Foundation to mistreat large numbers of people for no real purpose, while completely alleviating the affected parties of any moral culpability because it was all a compulsion. The note at the end, in addition to the problem of it being an overly dramatic "note at the end", compounds this by asking us to examine this from a moral standpoint when in fact there is no moral issue.

Even looking away from all that and putting the blinders on to see if this stands on its own, there is no purpose at all behind this evident other than "fuck with the Foundation." Now, I'm firmly of the belief that not everything needs backstory and purpose; those things can actually detract from otherwise good articles. But if something is going to be around the mess with the Foundation, and the Foundation specifically, there's got to be more to the story than "fuck you Foundation." Without motive, all it does is feel like unpleasant self-awareness on the part of the piece, a problem which this article already has.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was originally kind of meh on this, and yeah the nonstandard object class kind of irks me, but the interwiew was good enough to get me to upvote.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ehh, this seems weird. It is not bad, the article and the idea is rather pretty good. It's just… Not for me I guess. Definitely not in my headcannon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A fluid, later found to be cerebrospinal and amniotic

YIKES.

And while I agree with some of the criticisms raised in the discussion, this skip creeped me out so much I felt I had to upvote. There are so many other ways to, as someone said, diminish and eliminate empathy (destruction of the insula/amygdala, forcing false memories into the scientists' minds to make them believe they've committed these atrocities, etc) that even though I know this article is constrained by the existing D-class tropes, the Watsonian in me can't even begin to explain why 2959 would go to all this particular trouble with no reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you disagree, downvote and move on.

That's exactly what he was doing before he got jumped on for having an opinion contrary to the status quo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just a very on-the-nose amalgamation of blanket condemnations against how D-Class tend to be portrayed on this site without anything else really there. It would be a -J if it were funny. I wish you'd gone into, I don't know, something like "Maybe these few personnel are the only ones not infected, or maybe they're the only ones who are." Instead, it's just "Why would people treat people this way? It must be an anomaly!" Doesn't work for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh, my immediate thought upon seeing the contest announcement was "someone's gonna write an article where the D-Class as a whole is the SCP." It works, oddly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We always says Foundation is black and white, all the evil they have done are necesery.
But NO, it was white.

You are kicking people's ass!

I love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even in the light of all the evidence that detractors have posted and presented on here, even despite part of me somewhat agreeing that doing D-class as SCPs is kind of lazy, my very first gut reaction was "Oh. Fuck."

And I can't go against my first reaction to an article when it's that strong. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I downvoted, for reasons others have largely already intimated. In my impression, you've written a send-up of how horrifying and pointless the current D-class system is, and you've charted out the implications fairly well, but that's it.

I'm already largely against but somewhat agnostic towards the current D-class system, and I'm already aware of the horrific implications. The narrative doesn't extend anywhere beyond filling in the current system, except perhaps in the "Oh god, they're all infected, this horror will go on indefinitely", which I think is more a matter of emphasis than story.

It doesn't help that I read SCP-2193 right before this, which, although generally being perhaps less meticulously written and less horrific, went somewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I disagree with that assessment only because I think the way they're used in new articles is just as bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the interview is kinda interesting, the overall tone of this is just an anvilicious "hey, the way the D-Class are used in old Foundation articles is really bad, innit?". It is ultimately shallow and unnuanced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I haven't read this article yet, as I'm yet to go through the contest entries, but I didn't feel like letting some of the stuff in here stand unchallenged.

You're flipping right the hell out

No he's not. He's making an argument. You might find it fallacious, and that's fine. Stick to the facts of the matter. Don't characterize your opponents as hysterical because you don't like what they're saying.

There's little that's more annoying than people that decide that they have to tell a well-rated article how to "do it right". By definition, they already have.

If people like it and stuff what has it gets upvotes, then the site accepts its use. If you disagree, downvote and move on.

No. Absolutely not. Something being "well-rated" does not mean that it is by definition flawless and that anyone with something critical to say should shut up. Nothing is perfect. Nothing is so good that it couldn't benefit from any sort of commentary aside from "wow man you're awesome." This site is about writing the best stuff that we can, and going with this idea that once you've made it to the point where you can write something that sticks, you're now immune from any sort of criticism is counterproductive in the extreme.

If there's been a breach of conduct beyond the merits of an argument, staff will handle it. Until that point, it's not up to you to silence stuff because you don't like hearing it. "Positively rated" does not equal "perfection," and I will continue to approach the site in that manner.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can I just point out one thing?

You're flipping right the hell out over wanting a rigid enforcement of a single characteristic of the object categories, and seem to be placing yourself in a position of false authority regarding their use.

There are almost no hard rules. They are like a dictionary; the "rules" are really just a taxonomy of the most common conventions of the site (excepting things like anti-abuse or anti-spam rules, obviously).

There's little that's more annoying than people that decide that they have to tell a well-rated article how to "do it right". By definition, they already have. Focus on the ones that are struggling or need some spit and polish to live up to their potential, not ones that are doing quite well; that just makes you look foolish.

TL;DR: If people like it and stuff what has it gets upvotes, then the site accepts its use. If you disagree, downvote and move on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's by James Patrick Kelly. Really, really cool story.

Edit: Dang it, Ninja'd. One of these days I'll learn to hit refresh before replying to comments on pages I've had open all night.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's worse than that, in a way. Whatever these D-Class are, their side effect is to diminish, sometimes eliminate, empathy from a researcher or other Foundation personnel. If that doesn't sound horrifying, keep in mind that real-life researchers have been known to test the progress of syphilis in African-Americans and study the effects of ingesting radioactive material on war veterans and retarded children. And those were American doctors operating under government auspice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: James Patrick Kelly. It took home the Hugo for Best Novelette in 1996. Check the Seeing Ear Theatre website for an audio drama version, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh who wrote it? That sounds sweet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Don't disagree with you about clones/copies being worthy of humane treatment. Where the problem comes in… ever read the short story "Think like a Dinosaur"? Deals with a certain problem regarding teleporters, and ethical implications thereof. It's like that. One of a person is one thing. Two of the same person…things get grey for a number of reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IT still engenders cruelty. And just because they keep getting these people doesn't somehow make it okay to use them for things like this.

Edit: Being a copy doesn't mean they're not fully sapient people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good thing they have magically appearing copies of people so they don't have to use real people, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think there is any way to use people for scientific experimentation in the way the Foundation does and not be evil. To me, it very much is an issue of black and white.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's the thing, though. What you've got here is a black and white caricature that doesn't even consider the possibility that the D-class system could be anything other than a parade of sadism, and ignores the many modern portrayals of a D-class system without monthly terminations and inhumane treatment. That is to say, it ignores the idea that the Foundation is cold, not cruel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want a retraction, and I want some assurance that you'll give more of a shit about the truth before trash-talking people publicly.

An apology buried underneath a paragraph of baseless assumptions about my motivations (which I explained on a few occasions when the object classes were questioned) is completely worthless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The orientation is all lies. Lord help the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They're also infected. Very infected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So what does this say about the Ethics Committee? Are they infected or are they just doing a really bad job?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact of the matter is that you made a significant change to your articles months after they had been posted, a change that could be expected to have drawn downvotes on a newly created article. There is no universe where that doesn't look like tying to circumvent the normal voting process. If that wasn't your intent, then I'm sorry for misrepresenting it, but you have to understand what this kind of thing looks like from an outside perspective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, there have been many different ways people have made the D-class into anomalies. This article does a pretty good job on that front, in that these things just kind of hook up to a Foundation site and start turning personnel violent. As far as explaining some of the more lolFoundation treatment of D-class, that's actually kind of clever. Combined with the note from O5-5 which suggests that there is an active resistance against this anomaly, and this is a pretty solid article.

Thats not to say with it is without its flaws. The interview done by the CIA agent is rather clunky, as the interviewer come across as a rather stock hard-boiled agent type, which greatly detracts from the contrast between a regular person and D-class.

That aside, however, I still liked the article overall. +1 I can dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most of these things still have lives. They are sapient in every way. The five percent are just kind of… fucks up, factory missteps, even. Their anomalous nature doesn't change the cruelty being engendered in the Foundation and the cruelty of harming these people, imo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: People break the format all the time. Half the point of the format is to break it for dramatic effect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely agree with the general consensus here- the overall tone and structure are great, and I love the concept! The final message does get a little cliched but I wouldn't remove it entirely… I think it adds a nice conclusion to the article and does a good job of making it clear exactly what's going on (i.e. it isn't just one site with 'fake' D-class, it's all of them). Maybe just rewrite it a little bit?? All in all good work :>

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a concept that I originally wanted to do, but scrapped due to my lack of brains/talent. This is far better than anything I even imagined doing for this contest. It is executed wonderfully, and the final note is blissful icing on the cake. +1

Most experiments offer little scientific merit and achieve no greater understanding of the SCP object.

ETA: I literally squee'd here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with the other comments WRT the last memo, but that might just be personal preferences - I tend to go for skips where the horror comes from contrast of the clinical tone with whatever horrific thing is going on. It would have had that feel for me if you had cut it off after the interview log. I do understand the need to make it clear that O5s have been infected, though…maybe a higher security clearance over the whole document?

I also agree that Keter-potissimi is a little unnecessary tbqh but like….Does It Really Matter. [edit: i dont speak latin good so i had to look up what potissimus meant lmao.] [EDIT you put that at the top of the comments section oh man I need to read more carefully.] It's still a good article, and a concept so good it really should have been done by now. And honestly, I love the whole "they just materialize out of nowhere and nothing you tell anyone can convince them of that" biz. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding this, and adding that on a higher level it also steals quite a bit of the horror if the D-class aren't "real people". If they aren't even clones capable of long term survival, well, aside from the demotions thing… why do we care? It would totally eliminate the ethical issues, honestly.

Still undecided about my own vote…neutral for now, but leaning toward downvote.
Yeah, downvote. Not because it's badly written, because it isn't. It's just the more I think about it, the more problems it raises. Taken straight, it takes all of the drama out of the D-class and replaces it with a stock "the Foundation is infected by a thing and doesn't know it" anomaly. Even if taken as a thing that twists some individuals to see the D-class as pod people and their fellow Foundation members as insane, it still reduces the situation to the same old moral hand wringing rather than doing anything innovative with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any good work of fiction should make the audience question themselves. "What are my values?" "Would I act this way in this situation?" "What differentiates me from this character?"

This work does a very good job of that. It talks about prison labor, violence, peer pressure, the worth of a human life, and a lot more things, in what is not that many words.

I don't mind the Latin prefixes, I actually wish more people would use them. The object class is a VERY stale part of SCPs, especially nowadays, so some variety would be nice.

The weakest part of this skip by far is the last memo. It's just a bit much; trim it down a bit, maybe make it a bit more clinical in tone. This skip has brevity going for it, and you should run with that.

Anyway, this deserves my +1 a thousand times over. Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The interview does lean on the dark humour end for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
b) added after the skips had already been up for many months to try and avoid c) also attracted multiple downvotes.

You don't have to like the alt object classes, but that wasn't my motive at all and you don't have a good reason for asserting it. I didn't have the idea to do Latin object classes until quite a while after those articles were posted anyways.

If you have some issue with me, I recommend finding a method of settling it other than misrepresenting me publicly. If that's not the case, I recommend proceeding with more caution when accusing someone of subterfuge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is problematic.

On one hand this is a rather good and well established skip. The story went all right, and the overall article was worth to read. My only issues is that this is the safe option. I mean, turning D-class system into a skip is the closest thing to a safe shot in this contest. And while I honestly enjoyed it, the premise is somehow plain.

I have no problem upvoting it, but I'll do it later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You made the D-Class cliches horrifying.

+1 so hard

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How is this in any way funny?

Edit: actually, the doc thing is kinda funny, so nvm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know… the idea feels like a decent -J that tries to confront the D-class issue in its entirety. As a mainlister, I think it needs more work as to why the Foundation would justify the practice of going along with this practice (especially if you have subsumed the very usage of D-class personnel as an anomaly).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This has a lot in common with SCP-2130, "Office Furniture", which isn't really a bad thing as I consider that to be criminally underrated. I like the story you have here, although I feel the O5 note at the end is going slightly too far into cliche and could be better phrased in a different format.

That said, still worth my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just really don't understand the issue. I laid out why it would be there. It's like marking an object with a "priority" button. All Keter entails is that the object is quite hard to reliably contain. The addition of the adjectives add unique information to that object class, at a glance, that confers it as high priority. It's like a big red stamp. If you're not into it, it's fine, but please don't act as though I just put the thing in there for funsies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
By that logic, it shouldn't be that big of a deal to remove it then.

Single adjective or not, it's a format break. We have a format for a reason, and breaking from it needs to be justified.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's literally a single adjective, dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, and when Scantron did it it was a) also without reason b) added after the skips had already been up for many months to try and avoid c) also attracted multiple downvotes.

The fact of the matter is that this is intentionally breaking from format for on good reason. If this was better written, I might neutral vote. As it is, it isn't nearly good enough to get away with baseless format breaking.
